categories.name: Information Gathering
categories.summary: Information Gathering is the first phase of any security assessment, focused on collecting as much data about a target as possible before testing begins. This phase uses passive and active reconnaissance techniques to map the attack surface and identify potential weaknesses.
categories.intro: Information Gathering forms the foundation of web security testing. Before attempting to find vulnerabilities, testers must understand the target's technology stack, infrastructure, and exposed assets. This category covers techniques ranging from passive search engine reconnaissance to active fingerprinting of web servers and applications. Effective information gathering reduces wasted effort during later testing phases and often reveals low-hanging fruit like exposed admin panels, leaked credentials in source code, or misconfigured servers. For cert candidates, mastering this phase is essential because every penetration testing methodology starts here.

faq_items.q: What is WSTG-INFO and why is information gathering important in security testing?
faq_items.a: WSTG-INFO is the Information Gathering category of the OWASP Web Security Testing Guide. It covers techniques for collecting data about a target application before active testing begins. This phase is critical because it maps the attack surface, identifies technologies in use, and often reveals misconfigurations or exposed data that can be exploited in later phases.

faq_items.q: What is the difference between passive and active information gathering?
faq_items.a: Passive information gathering collects data without directly interacting with the target, using sources like search engines, DNS records, and public archives. Active information gathering involves direct interaction such as port scanning, fingerprinting, and crawling the application. Passive techniques are stealthier but provide less detail, while active techniques give more accurate results but may be detected.

faq_items.q: What tools are commonly used for web application information gathering?
faq_items.a: Common tools include Google Dorking for search engine reconnaissance, Nmap for network scanning, Wappalyzer and WhatWeb for technology fingerprinting, Shodan for internet-connected device discovery, and Burp Suite for mapping application structure. Many testers also use WHOIS lookups, DNS enumeration tools like dig and dnsenum, and web crawlers to build a complete picture of the target.

tests.name: Conduct Search Engine Discovery Reconnaissance
tests.description: This test uses search engines to discover publicly indexed information about a target, including sensitive files, exposed directories, and cached content. Search engine operators (Google Dorks) can reveal login pages, error messages, and documents that were never intended to be public.
tests.why_it_matters: Organizations often unknowingly expose sensitive information that gets indexed by search engines. Attackers routinely use search engine dorking as a zero-cost, zero-risk first step to find exploitable data.
what_to_check: Search for indexed admin panels and login pages using site: and inurl: operators
Look for exposed sensitive file types (PDFs, spreadsheets, configuration files) with filetype: operator
Check for cached versions of pages that may have been removed
Search for error messages or debug output that reveals technology stack details
Review robots.txt entries that might point to hidden directories

tests.name: Fingerprint Web Server
tests.description: Web server fingerprinting identifies the type and version of the web server software handling requests. This is done by analyzing HTTP response headers, default error pages, and server behavioral differences between implementations like Apache, Nginx, and IIS.
tests.why_it_matters: Knowing the exact web server version helps testers search for known vulnerabilities and misconfigurations specific to that software. It also reveals whether the server is running outdated or end-of-life software.
what_to_check: Examine the Server header in HTTP responses for version information
Analyze default error pages (404, 500) for server-specific formatting
Check HTTP response header ordering which differs between server implementations
Test how the server responds to malformed requests or unusual HTTP methods
Look for server-specific default files and directories

tests.name: Review Webserver Metafiles for Information Leakage
tests.description: Webserver metafiles like robots.txt, sitemap.xml, and security.txt can leak information about the application structure. These files often reveal hidden directories, admin paths, and internal endpoints that are not intended for public access.
tests.why_it_matters: Metafiles are publicly accessible by design but frequently contain references to sensitive areas of the application. Attackers use these files to quickly map out restricted areas and prioritize their testing efforts.
what_to_check: Review robots.txt for Disallow entries that point to sensitive directories
Check sitemap.xml for URLs not linked from the main application
Look for security.txt in the .well-known directory for contact and policy information
Search for humans.txt, crossdomain.xml, and clientaccesspolicy.xml
Check for exposed .git, .svn, or .env files in the web root

tests.name: Enumerate Applications on Webserver
tests.description: This test identifies all applications hosted on a web server, including those on non-standard ports, virtual hosts, and subdomains. A single server often hosts multiple applications, some of which may be forgotten, unmaintained, or running vulnerable software.
tests.why_it_matters: Forgotten or shadow applications are a common attack vector because they often lack patching and monitoring. Finding all applications on a server reveals the complete attack surface that needs to be tested.
what_to_check: Scan for applications running on non-standard ports
Enumerate virtual hosts using DNS records and brute-force techniques
Check for applications in common subdirectories (/admin, /portal, /api, /test)
Look for different applications served based on the Host header
Identify staging or development instances accessible from the internet

tests.name: Review Webpage Content for Information Leakage
tests.description: Reviewing webpage source code and content can reveal developer comments, internal IP addresses, API keys, debug information, and hidden form fields. This information is often left behind from development and provides valuable intelligence for attackers.
tests.why_it_matters: Developers frequently leave sensitive information in HTML comments, JavaScript files, and metadata that is invisible to regular users but easily found by anyone who views the source code. This leakage can expose internal infrastructure details or even credentials.
what_to_check: Search HTML source for developer comments containing sensitive information
Look for hardcoded API keys, tokens, or credentials in JavaScript files
Check for internal IP addresses or hostnames in page source
Review hidden form fields for parameters that control server-side logic
Examine metadata in documents and images for author names and software versions

tests.name: Identify Application Entry Points
tests.description: Identifying entry points means mapping every way data can enter the application, including URL parameters, form fields, HTTP headers, cookies, and API endpoints. Each entry point is a potential injection vector that needs to be tested in later phases.
tests.why_it_matters: Entry points define where user input interacts with application logic. Missing an entry point during testing means potentially missing a vulnerability. Thorough mapping ensures complete test coverage across the application.
what_to_check: Map all URL parameters across GET and POST requests
Identify form fields including hidden inputs
Document HTTP headers that influence application behavior (Host, Referer, User-Agent)
List all cookie values and their purposes
Catalog API endpoints and their accepted input formats (JSON, XML, multipart)

tests.name: Map Execution Paths Through Application
tests.description: Mapping execution paths traces how data flows through the application from input to output, including all processing steps, database queries, and external service calls. This helps identify where input validation occurs and where it might be bypassed.
tests.why_it_matters: Understanding data flow reveals where trust boundaries exist and where input might bypass validation. Complex execution paths with multiple processing stages often contain logic errors that can be exploited.
what_to_check: Trace how user input flows from entry point through processing to response
Identify trust boundaries where data crosses between components
Map multi-step processes like registration, checkout, or file upload workflows
Document conditional logic paths (error handling, edge cases, fallback behaviors)
Look for places where processed data is used in secondary operations

tests.name: Fingerprint Web Application Framework
tests.description: Framework fingerprinting identifies the web application framework (e.g., Django, Rails, Spring, Laravel) powering the target. This is accomplished by analyzing HTTP headers, cookie names, URL patterns, HTML structure, and error pages unique to each framework.
tests.why_it_matters: Each framework has known default configurations, common vulnerabilities, and specific attack patterns. Identifying the framework allows testers to focus on framework-specific issues and known CVEs rather than testing blindly.
what_to_check: Analyze cookie names for framework-specific patterns (JSESSIONID for Java, PHPSESSID for PHP)
Check HTTP response headers for framework-specific identifiers (X-Powered-By, X-AspNet-Version)
Examine URL structures and routing patterns characteristic of specific frameworks
Look for default error pages, login screens, or admin panels with framework branding
Check for framework-specific files in predictable locations

tests.name: Fingerprint Web Application
tests.description: This test identifies the specific web application or CMS in use, such as WordPress, Drupal, or a custom application. It goes beyond framework identification to determine the exact software product and version, including plugins and extensions.
tests.why_it_matters: Known applications have documented vulnerability databases, default credentials, and established exploitation techniques. Identifying the exact version allows testers to check for unpatched CVEs and known attack paths.
what_to_check: Check for CMS-specific files (wp-login.php for WordPress, /administrator for Joomla)
Review HTML source for generator meta tags and version comments
Fingerprint installed plugins and extensions through their public-facing resources
Analyze JavaScript and CSS file paths for application-specific naming conventions
Compare page structure and behavior against known application signatures

tests.name: Map Application Architecture
tests.description: Architecture mapping builds a complete picture of the target's infrastructure including web servers, application servers, databases, load balancers, CDNs, WAFs, and third-party services. Understanding the full architecture reveals how components interact and where security boundaries exist.
tests.why_it_matters: Modern applications rely on complex architectures with multiple components. Knowing the full architecture helps testers identify weak links, bypass security controls at component boundaries, and understand the impact of potential vulnerabilities.
what_to_check: Identify reverse proxies, load balancers, and CDN providers from response headers
Detect Web Application Firewalls (WAFs) that may filter attack payloads
Map backend databases and storage services from error messages and behavior
Document third-party integrations and API dependencies
Identify different environments (production, staging, development) accessible from the internet

categories.name: Configuration and Deployment Management
categories.summary: Configuration and Deployment Management testing examines how securely the application infrastructure is configured and maintained. Misconfigurations are among the most common vulnerabilities and often provide the easiest path for attackers to gain unauthorized access.
categories.intro: Secure configuration is a cornerstone of application security, yet it is frequently overlooked. This category tests whether web servers, application platforms, and cloud services are deployed with hardened settings rather than insecure defaults. Misconfiguration issues range from exposed admin interfaces and verbose error messages to missing security headers and overly permissive file permissions. These issues are particularly dangerous because they often require no authentication to exploit and can be found through automated scanning. For security professionals, understanding configuration hardening is essential for both offensive testing and defensive architecture reviews.

faq_items.q: What are the most common web server misconfigurations that lead to security vulnerabilities?
faq_items.a: The most common misconfigurations include default credentials on admin interfaces, directory listing enabled, verbose error messages exposing stack traces, missing security headers (HSTS, CSP, X-Frame-Options), unnecessary HTTP methods enabled (PUT, DELETE, TRACE), and outdated TLS configurations. These issues are easy to find and easy to fix, which is why they appear so frequently in security assessments.

faq_items.q: How does WSTG-CONF relate to the OWASP Top 10?
faq_items.a: WSTG-CONF maps directly to OWASP Top 10 A05: Security Misconfiguration, which covers missing security hardening, unnecessary features enabled, default accounts, overly informative error messages, and misconfigured security headers. It is consistently one of the most prevalent vulnerability categories found in real-world applications.

faq_items.q: What is subdomain takeover and why is it a configuration issue?
faq_items.a: Subdomain takeover occurs when a DNS record points to a service (like a cloud hosting platform) that the organization no longer controls. An attacker can claim the orphaned service and serve malicious content under the organization's domain. This is a configuration management issue because it results from failing to clean up DNS records when decommissioning services.

tests.name: Test Network Infrastructure Configuration
tests.description: This test evaluates the security configuration of network infrastructure components including firewalls, routers, load balancers, and DNS servers. It checks for unnecessary open ports, weak access controls, and insecure protocols that could provide an entry point into the environment.
tests.why_it_matters: Network infrastructure misconfigurations can bypass application-level security controls entirely. An attacker who finds an open management port or insecure protocol can often gain deeper access than through the web application itself.
what_to_check: Scan for unnecessary open ports and services on the server
Verify that administrative interfaces are not publicly accessible
Check for insecure protocols (Telnet, FTP, unencrypted SNMP)
Test DNS configuration for zone transfer vulnerabilities
Review firewall rules for overly permissive configurations

tests.name: Test Application Platform Configuration
tests.description: This test reviews the configuration of application platforms such as web servers, application servers, and database servers. It checks for insecure default settings, unnecessary features, debug modes left enabled, and missing security hardening measures.
tests.why_it_matters: Application platforms are often deployed with default configurations optimized for development convenience rather than security. Debug modes, sample applications, and default accounts left in production create easy targets for attackers.
what_to_check: Check for debug mode or verbose error configuration in production
Verify that default or sample applications have been removed
Review logging configuration for sensitive data exposure
Test that directory listing is disabled on the web server
Confirm unnecessary modules and features are disabled

tests.name: Test File Extensions Handling for Sensitive Information
tests.description: This test examines how the web server handles different file extensions, particularly whether it exposes source code or sensitive files when requested with specific extensions. Mishandled extensions can cause the server to serve raw source code instead of executing it.
tests.why_it_matters: If a web server serves source code files instead of executing them, attackers can read application logic, database credentials, API keys, and internal comments. This often happens with backup files (.bak, .old) or alternative extensions (.php~, .asp.old).
what_to_check: Request files with backup extensions (.bak, .old, .orig, .copy, .tmp)
Test for source code disclosure using tilde (~) or editor-generated extensions
Check if the server handles file extension case sensitivity correctly
Look for configuration files accessible through extension manipulation
Verify that server-side script extensions are always processed, never served raw

tests.name: Review Old Backup and Unreferenced Files
tests.description: This test searches for old backup files, unreferenced pages, and leftover development artifacts on the web server. These files often contain sensitive information, source code, or older versions of the application with known vulnerabilities.
tests.why_it_matters: Backup files and unreferenced content frequently contain credentials, configuration details, or outdated code with known security issues. Because these files are not linked from the main application, they often escape security reviews and remain accessible indefinitely.
what_to_check: Brute-force common backup file patterns (.zip, .tar.gz, .sql, .bak for each known file)
Search for version control artifacts (.git, .svn, .hg directories)
Look for database dumps and SQL backup files
Check for IDE and editor temporary files (.swp, .swo, ~files)
Scan for unreferenced administrative or test pages

tests.name: Enumerate Infrastructure and Application Admin Interfaces
tests.description: This test identifies administrative interfaces for both infrastructure components and the application itself. Admin panels often provide elevated access and may be protected only by obscurity rather than proper authentication and access controls.
tests.why_it_matters: Administrative interfaces are high-value targets because they typically provide full control over the application or infrastructure. When these are exposed with default credentials or weak authentication, they represent a critical risk to the entire system.
what_to_check: Scan for common admin panel paths (/admin, /administrator, /manage, /console)
Check for infrastructure management interfaces (phpMyAdmin, Tomcat Manager, cPanel)
Look for admin interfaces on non-standard ports
Test discovered admin panels for default credentials
Verify that admin interfaces enforce strong authentication and IP restrictions

tests.name: Test HTTP Methods
tests.description: This test checks which HTTP methods the web server accepts and whether dangerous methods like PUT, DELETE, or TRACE are enabled. Unnecessary HTTP methods can allow attackers to upload malicious files, delete content, or perform cross-site tracing attacks.
tests.why_it_matters: Enabling unnecessary HTTP methods expands the attack surface without providing value. The TRACE method can be used for cross-site tracing attacks, while PUT and DELETE can allow unauthorized file manipulation if access controls are not properly configured.
what_to_check: Send an OPTIONS request to enumerate allowed HTTP methods
Test if PUT method allows arbitrary file uploads
Check if DELETE method can remove files from the server
Verify TRACE method is disabled to prevent cross-site tracing (XST)
Test for HTTP method override headers (X-HTTP-Method-Override, X-Method-Override)

tests.name: Test HTTP Strict Transport Security
tests.description: This test verifies that HTTP Strict Transport Security (HSTS) is properly implemented to force browsers to only connect via HTTPS. Without HSTS, users may be vulnerable to SSL stripping attacks where an attacker downgrades the connection from HTTPS to HTTP.
tests.why_it_matters: HSTS prevents SSL stripping and protocol downgrade attacks by instructing browsers to always use HTTPS. Without it, an attacker on the same network can intercept the initial HTTP request before the redirect to HTTPS and hijack the session.
what_to_check: Verify the Strict-Transport-Security header is present in HTTPS responses
Check that the max-age value is sufficiently long (at least 31536000 seconds / 1 year)
Confirm includeSubDomains directive is set if subdomains should also use HTTPS
Check if the domain is in the HSTS preload list for maximum protection
Test that HTTP requests properly redirect to HTTPS before serving content

tests.name: Test RIA Cross Domain Policy
tests.description: This test examines cross-domain policy files (crossdomain.xml for Flash and clientaccesspolicy.xml for Silverlight) that control which external domains can interact with the application. Overly permissive policies can allow any domain to make cross-origin requests.
tests.why_it_matters: While Flash and Silverlight are largely deprecated, cross-domain policy files may still be present on servers and could be exploited if overly permissive. A wildcard policy allowing all domains effectively disables the same-origin policy for the application.
what_to_check: Check for the existence of crossdomain.xml in the web root
Check for clientaccesspolicy.xml in the web root
Verify that policies do not use wildcard (*) domain permissions
Ensure policies restrict allowed domains to trusted partners only
Confirm that policies specify secure=true for HTTPS-only access

tests.name: Test File Permission
tests.description: This test reviews file and directory permissions on the web server to ensure sensitive files are not world-readable and that upload directories do not allow script execution. Incorrect permissions can expose configuration files, logs, and credentials.
tests.why_it_matters: Improperly configured file permissions can allow unauthorized users to read sensitive configuration files, write malicious content, or execute uploaded scripts. This is especially critical in shared hosting environments where multiple applications run on the same server.
what_to_check: Verify configuration files are not readable by the web server user
Check that log files are not accessible through the web server
Ensure upload directories do not have execute permissions
Review file ownership to confirm principle of least privilege
Test for directory traversal paths that might access restricted files

tests.name: Test for Subdomain Takeover
tests.description: Subdomain takeover occurs when a DNS record (usually a CNAME) points to an external service that the organization no longer controls. An attacker can register the abandoned service and serve content under the organization's trusted domain.
tests.why_it_matters: Subdomain takeover allows attackers to host malicious content on a trusted domain, which can be used for phishing, cookie theft, or bypassing content security policies. It exploits the trust users and browsers place in the organization's domain.
what_to_check: Enumerate all subdomains and their DNS records (A, CNAME, MX)
Identify CNAME records pointing to external services (AWS, Azure, GitHub Pages, Heroku)
Check if the target services return error pages indicating unclaimed resources
Verify that decommissioned services have had their DNS records removed
Test if you can register the orphaned service endpoint

tests.name: Test Cloud Storage
tests.description: This test checks whether cloud storage services (S3 buckets, Azure Blobs, Google Cloud Storage) associated with the application are securely configured. Misconfigured cloud storage is one of the most common causes of large-scale data breaches.
tests.why_it_matters: Cloud storage misconfigurations have caused some of the largest data breaches in history. Publicly readable or writable buckets can expose millions of records, and these misconfigurations can be found through simple enumeration and guessing of bucket names.
what_to_check: Enumerate cloud storage endpoints from application source code and DNS records
Test S3 buckets for public read access (ListBucket, GetObject)
Check for public write permissions that could allow data modification
Verify that bucket policies and ACLs follow the principle of least privilege
Look for cloud storage URLs in JavaScript files, error messages, and metadata

categories.name: Identity Management
categories.summary: Identity Management testing evaluates how the application creates, manages, and controls user identities. Weaknesses in identity management can lead to unauthorized account creation, privilege escalation, and account takeover attacks.
categories.intro: Identity Management is the foundation upon which authentication and authorization are built. This category examines the processes by which user accounts are created, roles are assigned, and identities are managed throughout their lifecycle. Testing in this area reveals whether an attacker can create unauthorized accounts, enumerate valid usernames, escalate privileges through role manipulation, or exploit weak identity policies. Understanding identity management vulnerabilities is critical for both offensive security testing and secure application design. These tests are highly relevant for certifications like CISSP, which emphasizes Identity and Access Management as a core security domain.

faq_items.q: What is identity management in web application security?
faq_items.a: Identity management in web security refers to how applications handle user accounts throughout their lifecycle: registration, provisioning, role assignment, and deactivation. Poor identity management can allow attackers to create fake accounts, enumerate valid users, or gain elevated privileges through flawed role assignment processes.

faq_items.q: What is account enumeration and why is it a security risk?
faq_items.a: Account enumeration occurs when an application reveals whether a specific username or email exists in the system, typically through different error messages on login, registration, or password reset pages. This is risky because attackers can compile a list of valid accounts to target with brute-force attacks, credential stuffing, or social engineering campaigns.

faq_items.q: How do identity management vulnerabilities differ from authentication vulnerabilities?
faq_items.a: Identity management focuses on how accounts and roles are created and maintained, while authentication focuses on how users prove their identity. For example, a weak registration process that allows duplicate accounts is an identity management issue, while a weak password policy is an authentication issue. Both are critical, but they target different parts of the access control chain.

tests.name: Test Role Definitions
tests.description: This test evaluates how the application defines and enforces user roles. It examines whether roles follow the principle of least privilege, whether role definitions are clear, and whether there are ways to manipulate role assignments to gain elevated access.
tests.why_it_matters: Poorly defined roles or overly permissive default role assignments can give users more access than intended. This is a common root cause of privilege escalation vulnerabilities and data breaches involving insider threats.
what_to_check: Identify all user roles and their associated permissions
Verify that roles follow the principle of least privilege
Test whether role assignments can be manipulated through client-side parameters
Check for overlapping permissions between roles that could create confusion
Confirm that administrative roles are properly separated from regular user roles

tests.name: Test User Registration Process
tests.description: This test examines the user registration process for weaknesses including lack of CAPTCHA, acceptance of disposable email addresses, weak identity verification, and the ability to register with duplicate or spoofed identities.
tests.why_it_matters: A weak registration process enables attackers to create fraudulent accounts at scale for spam, abuse, or as stepping stones for further attacks. It also affects data integrity if duplicate or fake identities can pollute the user base.
what_to_check: Test if registration can be automated without CAPTCHA or rate limiting
Check if disposable email addresses are accepted for registration
Verify that duplicate accounts with the same email or username are rejected
Test whether registration parameters can be manipulated to set elevated roles
Confirm email verification is required before account activation

tests.name: Test Account Provisioning Process
tests.description: This test reviews how accounts are provisioned, modified, and deprovisioned within the application. It checks whether administrative account management follows secure processes and whether former users retain access after deprovisioning.
tests.why_it_matters: Improper account provisioning can leave orphaned accounts with active credentials, allow unauthorized privilege changes, or fail to revoke access when employees leave. This is a major concern for compliance frameworks like SOC 2 and ISO 27001.
what_to_check: Verify that account creation follows an approval workflow for privileged roles
Test whether deprovisioned accounts are fully disabled and cannot authenticate
Check if account modification actions are logged and auditable
Confirm that temporary or test accounts have expiration dates
Test whether bulk account provisioning properly validates each account

tests.name: Testing for Account Enumeration and Guessable User Account
tests.description: This test determines whether an attacker can enumerate valid usernames or email addresses through the application's login, registration, or password recovery functions. Different error messages, response times, or HTTP status codes for valid versus invalid accounts reveal this information.
tests.why_it_matters: Account enumeration gives attackers a list of valid targets for credential stuffing, brute-force, and social engineering attacks. Even subtle differences in responses can be used to distinguish valid from invalid accounts at scale.
what_to_check: Compare error messages for valid versus invalid usernames on the login page
Check registration forms for different responses when an existing email is used
Test password reset functionality for account enumeration through error messages
Measure response time differences between valid and invalid username attempts
Look for differences in HTTP response codes or headers between valid and invalid attempts

tests.name: Testing for Weak or Unenforced Username Policy
tests.description: This test evaluates whether the application enforces a strong username policy, including minimum length, allowed characters, and uniqueness requirements. Weak username policies can make accounts easier to guess and harder to manage securely.
tests.why_it_matters: Weak username policies allow predictable account names (admin, test, user1) that attackers can easily guess. Combined with weak passwords, predictable usernames make credential attacks trivially easy.
what_to_check: Test if the application allows very short or single-character usernames
Check whether common reserved names (admin, root, test) can be registered by regular users
Verify that username format requirements are enforced consistently
Test for case sensitivity in username handling (Admin vs admin)
Check if usernames can contain special characters that might cause injection issues

categories.name: Authentication Testing
categories.summary: Authentication Testing verifies how securely the application confirms user identities. Weak authentication mechanisms are among the most exploited vulnerabilities, enabling account takeover, credential theft, and unauthorized access to sensitive data.
categories.intro: Authentication is the gatekeeper of application security. This category tests every aspect of how users prove their identity, from password policies and credential storage to multi-factor authentication and session management during login. Weak authentication is consistently ranked among the top web application risks because it provides direct access to user accounts and sensitive data. Tests in this category cover credential transport security, brute-force protections, password reset flows, and authentication bypass techniques. For security professionals and cert candidates, deep understanding of authentication weaknesses is essential because these vulnerabilities have the most direct business impact.

faq_items.q: What are the most critical authentication vulnerabilities in web applications?
faq_items.a: The most critical authentication vulnerabilities include default credentials, weak password policies, missing brute-force protections, insecure password reset mechanisms, and authentication bypass through parameter manipulation. These are commonly exploited because they provide direct access to user accounts and often require minimal technical skill to exploit.

faq_items.q: How does WSTG-ATHN differ from WSTG-IDNT and WSTG-SESS?
faq_items.a: WSTG-IDNT tests how accounts are created and managed, WSTG-ATHN tests how users prove their identity when logging in, and WSTG-SESS tests how that authenticated state is maintained after login. Together, they cover the full lifecycle of user access: creation, verification, and session persistence.

faq_items.q: What is credential stuffing and how does it relate to authentication testing?
faq_items.a: Credential stuffing uses username/password pairs leaked from data breaches on other sites to attempt logins on the target application. It exploits password reuse across services. Authentication testing evaluates whether the application has defenses against this attack, including rate limiting, account lockout policies, CAPTCHA challenges, and multi-factor authentication.

tests.name: Testing for Credentials Transported over an Encrypted Channel
tests.description: This test verifies that user credentials (usernames and passwords) are always transmitted over encrypted channels (HTTPS/TLS). Credentials sent over unencrypted HTTP can be intercepted by anyone on the network path between the user and the server.
tests.why_it_matters: Transmitting credentials over unencrypted connections exposes them to network-level interception through packet sniffing, man-in-the-middle attacks, or compromised network infrastructure. This is especially dangerous on public Wi-Fi networks.
what_to_check: Verify the login form is served over HTTPS and submits to an HTTPS endpoint
Check that HTTP-to-HTTPS redirects happen before credentials are submitted
Test whether login forms can be loaded over HTTP by directly entering the URL
Confirm that API authentication endpoints require HTTPS
Verify that HSTS is configured to prevent protocol downgrade attacks

tests.name: Testing for Default Credentials
tests.description: This test checks whether the application, its infrastructure, or any administrative interfaces still use default credentials that were set during installation. Default credentials are publicly known and are one of the first things attackers try.
tests.why_it_matters: Default credentials are published in vendor documentation, security databases, and hacking tools. They represent zero-effort access for attackers and are frequently found in network devices, databases, CMS platforms, and administrative panels.
what_to_check: Test common default username/password combinations for the identified platform
Check for admin accounts with well-known defaults (admin/admin, admin/password)
Review vendor documentation for default credentials specific to the software version
Test database management interfaces for default database credentials
Check for service accounts with default or blank passwords

tests.name: Testing for Weak Lock Out Mechanism
tests.description: This test evaluates whether the application has adequate brute-force protection through account lockout mechanisms, rate limiting, or progressive delays. It also checks whether the lockout mechanism itself can be abused to deny legitimate users access.
tests.why_it_matters: Without lockout mechanisms, attackers can attempt unlimited password guesses until they find the correct one. However, poorly implemented lockouts can be exploited to lock out legitimate users (denial of service) or bypassed through techniques like IP rotation or credential stuffing.
what_to_check: Determine how many failed attempts trigger an account lockout
Test whether lockout can be bypassed by changing IP addresses
Check if lockout applies to all authentication methods (web, API, mobile)
Verify that lockout notifications are sent to the account owner
Test whether an attacker can intentionally lock out other users' accounts

tests.name: Testing for Bypassing Authentication Schema
tests.description: This test attempts to bypass the authentication mechanism entirely through techniques like forced browsing, parameter manipulation, SQL injection in login forms, session token prediction, or exploiting flaws in the authentication logic itself.
tests.why_it_matters: Authentication bypass is one of the most severe vulnerabilities because it grants full unauthorized access without needing valid credentials. These flaws often stem from client-side validation, predictable session tokens, or logic errors in the authentication flow.
what_to_check: Test forced browsing to authenticated pages without logging in first
Manipulate authentication parameters (user role, isAdmin, authentication tokens)
Test SQL injection in username and password fields
Check for authentication bypass through HTTP header manipulation
Test if modifying the response from the server can trick the client into granting access

tests.name: Testing for Vulnerable Remember Password
tests.description: This test examines how the application implements 'remember me' or 'stay logged in' functionality. Insecure implementations may store credentials in cookies, use predictable tokens, or fail to provide adequate expiration and revocation mechanisms.
tests.why_it_matters: Insecure 'remember me' implementations can store credentials in plaintext cookies, use predictable token values that attackers can guess, or create long-lived sessions that remain valid even after password changes. These persist the risk of account compromise beyond a single session.
what_to_check: Examine remember-me cookies for plaintext or reversible credential storage
Test whether remember-me tokens are cryptographically random and unpredictable
Verify that remember-me tokens are invalidated on password change
Check if remember-me functionality has a reasonable expiration time
Test whether remember-me tokens are bound to the original client or can be replayed

tests.name: Testing for Browser Cache Weaknesses
tests.description: This test checks whether sensitive data from authenticated pages is stored in the browser cache, history, or back/forward cache. Cached sensitive content can be accessed by subsequent users of a shared computer or recovered through forensic analysis.
tests.why_it_matters: On shared or public computers, browser cache can expose sensitive data to the next user. Even on personal devices, cached credentials and sensitive pages can be extracted through malware or physical access to the device.
what_to_check: Check for Cache-Control and Pragma headers preventing caching of sensitive pages
Verify that the back button does not display authenticated content after logout
Test whether autocomplete is disabled on sensitive form fields (credentials, SSN, credit card)
Check browser history for sensitive URL parameters
Verify that no-store directive prevents disk caching of sensitive responses

tests.name: Testing for Weak Password Policy
tests.description: This test evaluates the application's password policy for minimum complexity requirements, length restrictions, and whether it prevents commonly used passwords. A weak password policy makes brute-force and dictionary attacks much more likely to succeed.
tests.why_it_matters: Weak passwords are the most common cause of account compromise. Applications that allow short, simple, or commonly used passwords make it easy for attackers to guess credentials through automated attacks, especially when combined with account enumeration.
what_to_check: Test minimum and maximum password length requirements
Check if the application enforces complexity rules (uppercase, lowercase, numbers, special characters)
Verify that commonly used passwords (password123, qwerty, letmein) are rejected
Test if the application checks passwords against known breach databases
Confirm that password history prevents reuse of recent passwords

tests.name: Testing for Weak Security Question Answer
tests.description: This test evaluates the strength of security questions used for account recovery or additional authentication. Weak security questions have answers that are easily guessed, publicly available, or the same for many users.
tests.why_it_matters: Security questions are often the weakest link in account recovery. Answers to common questions like 'mother's maiden name' or 'first pet' can be found through social media research, public records, or simple guessing, allowing attackers to bypass authentication entirely.
what_to_check: Evaluate whether security questions have easily guessable or researchable answers
Test if the application allows users to set their own questions with trivial answers
Check if security question answers are case-sensitive
Verify that brute-force protection applies to security question attempts
Test whether security questions can be bypassed through other recovery methods

tests.name: Testing for Weak Password Change or Reset Functionalities
tests.description: This test examines the password change and reset mechanisms for weaknesses including insecure reset tokens, lack of current password verification during changes, and exploitable password recovery flows that could enable account takeover.
tests.why_it_matters: Password reset mechanisms are a prime target for account takeover attacks. Predictable reset tokens, tokens sent via insecure channels, or reset flows that do not properly verify identity can give attackers full control of any user account.
what_to_check: Test whether password change requires the current password for verification
Analyze password reset tokens for randomness and predictability
Verify that reset tokens expire within a reasonable timeframe
Check if reset tokens are single-use and invalidated after password change
Test for host header injection in password reset emails

tests.name: Testing for Weaker Authentication in Alternative Channel
tests.description: This test checks whether alternative authentication channels (mobile app, API, legacy interfaces, partner portals) have weaker security controls than the primary web application. Attackers often target the weakest authentication path available.
tests.why_it_matters: Organizations often invest heavily in securing their primary web login but overlook alternative channels that access the same backend. A mobile API with no rate limiting or a legacy portal without MFA can undermine the security of the entire system.
what_to_check: Identify all authentication channels (web, mobile API, legacy systems, partner portals)
Compare authentication strength across channels (password policy, MFA, lockout)
Test if credentials are shared across channels with different security levels
Check if alternative channels bypass security controls enforced on the primary channel
Verify that all channels enforce the same session management policies

categories.name: Authorization Testing
categories.summary: Authorization Testing evaluates whether the application properly enforces access controls after a user has been authenticated. These tests check if users can access resources, perform actions, or view data that should be restricted to other roles or users.
categories.intro: Authorization determines what an authenticated user is allowed to do within an application. Unlike authentication (verifying who you are), authorization controls what you can access. This category tests for broken access controls including directory traversal, privilege escalation, insecure direct object references, and authorization bypass. Authorization vulnerabilities are the number one risk in the OWASP Top 10 (A01:2021) because they are widespread, often application-specific, and difficult to detect with automated tools. They require understanding the application's business logic and testing with multiple user roles. For penetration testers and bug bounty hunters, authorization testing is one of the most rewarding areas because these findings often have high severity and direct business impact.

faq_items.q: What is the difference between authentication and authorization in web security?
faq_items.a: Authentication verifies a user's identity (who are you?), while authorization determines what that user is allowed to do (what can you access?). A user can be properly authenticated but still access resources they should not be authorized to view. Authorization vulnerabilities are ranked as the number one web application security risk by OWASP.

faq_items.q: What are Insecure Direct Object References (IDOR) and why are they so common?
faq_items.a: IDOR vulnerabilities occur when an application uses user-supplied input to directly access objects (like database records or files) without verifying that the user is authorized to access that specific object. They are common because developers often implement authentication but forget to add object-level authorization checks, assuming that if a user is logged in, they should have access to any resource they can reference.

faq_items.q: How do you test for privilege escalation in web applications?
faq_items.a: Privilege escalation testing involves attempting to access higher-privilege functionality while logged in as a lower-privilege user. Horizontal escalation means accessing another user's data at the same privilege level, while vertical escalation means gaining admin-level access from a regular user account. Testers use techniques like changing user IDs in requests, modifying role parameters, and accessing admin endpoints with regular user tokens.

tests.name: Testing Directory Traversal File Include
tests.description: Directory traversal (path traversal) testing attempts to access files outside the intended directory by manipulating file path parameters with sequences like ../ or URL-encoded equivalents. Successful exploitation can read sensitive system files, configuration files, or application source code.
tests.why_it_matters: Directory traversal can expose any file the web server process has permission to read, including /etc/passwd, application configuration files with database credentials, and source code. Combined with file inclusion vulnerabilities, it can lead to remote code execution.
what_to_check: Test parameters that reference files with ../ sequences to escape the intended directory
Try URL-encoded and double-encoded traversal sequences (%2e%2e%2f, %252e%252e%252f)
Attempt to read known system files (/etc/passwd on Linux, C:\boot.ini on Windows)
Test for null byte injection to bypass file extension restrictions
Check for Local File Inclusion (LFI) and Remote File Inclusion (RFI) in include parameters

tests.name: Testing for Bypassing Authorization Schema
tests.description: This test attempts to bypass the application's authorization controls to access pages, functions, or data that should be restricted. It tests whether authorization is enforced consistently on both the client and server side.
tests.why_it_matters: Authorization bypass is the most common and impactful access control vulnerability. If authorization is only enforced in the UI (hiding buttons or menu items) but not on the server, attackers can directly request restricted resources and gain unauthorized access.
what_to_check: Access administrative URLs directly while logged in as a regular user
Replay requests captured from a privileged session using a lower-privilege session token
Test whether authorization checks apply to both GET and POST requests for the same resource
Modify request parameters that indicate user role or permission level
Check if removing authorization headers or cookies allows access to restricted endpoints

tests.name: Testing for Privilege Escalation
tests.description: Privilege escalation testing checks whether a user can elevate their access level within the application. Vertical escalation gains higher-privilege access (user to admin), while horizontal escalation accesses another user's data at the same level.
tests.why_it_matters: Privilege escalation undermines the entire access control model. Vertical escalation gives attackers administrative control, while horizontal escalation exposes other users' personal data. Both represent critical security failures with direct compliance and liability implications.
what_to_check: Test vertical escalation by accessing admin functions with a regular user session
Test horizontal escalation by changing user identifiers in requests to access other users' data
Modify role or group parameters in POST requests during account updates
Check if API endpoints enforce the same authorization as the web interface
Test whether modifying JWT claims or session attributes can elevate privileges

tests.name: Testing for Insecure Direct Object References
tests.description: IDOR testing checks whether changing a reference to an object (like an ID, filename, or key in a URL or request body) allows access to another user's data without authorization checks. This is one of the most commonly found and easily exploited web vulnerabilities.
tests.why_it_matters: IDOR vulnerabilities provide direct access to other users' private data, accounts, or resources. They are extremely common because developers often check if a user is authenticated but fail to verify they are authorized to access the specific object being requested.
what_to_check: Modify numeric IDs in URLs and request parameters to access other users' records
Test file download endpoints by changing filenames or document IDs
Check API responses for references to objects belonging to other users
Test sequential and predictable identifiers versus random UUIDs
Verify authorization on all CRUD operations (Create, Read, Update, Delete) for each object

tests.name: Testing for OAuth Weaknesses
tests.description: This test evaluates OAuth implementations for common security weaknesses including open redirectors in the redirect_uri, authorization code reuse, missing state parameter validation, and insecure token storage. OAuth complexity creates many opportunities for implementation errors.
tests.why_it_matters: OAuth is widely used for third-party authentication and authorization, but its complexity leads to frequent implementation flaws. Vulnerabilities in OAuth flows can result in account takeover, unauthorized data access, and token theft, affecting both the application and its users.
what_to_check: Test for open redirect vulnerabilities in the redirect_uri parameter
Verify the state parameter is used and validated to prevent CSRF attacks
Check if authorization codes can be reused or are properly single-use
Test whether access tokens are transmitted and stored securely
Verify that scope escalation is not possible through parameter manipulation

categories.name: Session Management Testing
categories.summary: Session Management Testing examines how the application creates, maintains, and destroys user sessions. Weak session management can lead to session hijacking, session fixation, and cross-site request forgery, allowing attackers to impersonate legitimate users.
categories.intro: Session management bridges the gap between authentication and the ongoing user experience. After a user logs in, the application must maintain their authenticated state across multiple requests using session tokens, cookies, or other mechanisms. This category tests whether session identifiers are generated securely, transmitted safely, protected against fixation and hijacking, and properly invalidated on logout. Session management vulnerabilities are particularly dangerous because they allow attackers to take over authenticated sessions without needing the user's credentials. For security professionals, understanding session management is crucial because flaws in this area directly undermine authentication controls, regardless of how strong those controls might be.

faq_items.q: What is session hijacking and how does it work?
faq_items.a: Session hijacking occurs when an attacker obtains a valid session token belonging to another user and uses it to impersonate that user. This can happen through network sniffing (if cookies lack the Secure flag), cross-site scripting (if cookies lack HttpOnly), session fixation, or physical access to the user's browser. Once the attacker has the session token, the server cannot distinguish them from the legitimate user.

faq_items.q: What is session fixation and how is it different from session hijacking?
faq_items.a: Session fixation is an attack where the attacker sets (fixes) the session ID before the victim logs in, rather than stealing it after. The attacker provides a known session ID to the victim (through a crafted URL or cookie injection), waits for the victim to authenticate, and then uses that same session ID to access the authenticated session. Prevention requires regenerating the session ID after successful login.

faq_items.q: What cookie security attributes should be set on session cookies?
faq_items.a: Session cookies should have the Secure flag (transmitted only over HTTPS), HttpOnly flag (inaccessible to JavaScript, preventing XSS-based theft), SameSite attribute (Strict or Lax, preventing CSRF), appropriate Path and Domain restrictions, and a reasonable expiration. Missing any of these attributes weakens session security.

tests.name: Testing for Session Management Schema
tests.description: This test evaluates the overall session management implementation including how session tokens are generated, their randomness, length, and whether they contain embedded sensitive information. It also checks the session lifecycle from creation through destruction.
tests.why_it_matters: The session management schema is the foundation of maintaining authenticated state. If session tokens are predictable, too short, or contain embedded user information, attackers can forge, guess, or extract session tokens to impersonate users.
what_to_check: Analyze session token length, character set, and entropy
Test for predictable patterns by collecting and comparing multiple session tokens
Check if session tokens contain encoded user data (Base64 decode to inspect)
Verify that new session tokens are generated after authentication events
Confirm the session management mechanism is resistant to token prediction attacks

tests.name: Testing for Cookies Attributes
tests.description: This test examines the security attributes set on session cookies, including Secure, HttpOnly, SameSite, Domain, Path, and Expires/Max-Age flags. Missing or misconfigured cookie attributes can expose session tokens to theft through various attack vectors.
tests.why_it_matters: Cookie attributes are the primary defense against session token theft. Without the Secure flag, tokens leak over HTTP connections. Without HttpOnly, XSS can steal them. Without SameSite, CSRF attacks can misuse them. Each missing attribute opens a different attack path.
what_to_check: Verify the Secure flag is set to prevent transmission over HTTP
Confirm HttpOnly flag is set to prevent JavaScript access
Check SameSite attribute is set to Strict or Lax to prevent CSRF
Review Domain and Path attributes for overly broad scope
Verify appropriate Expires or Max-Age values are set

tests.name: Testing for Session Fixation
tests.description: Session fixation testing checks whether the application regenerates the session identifier after successful authentication. Without regeneration, an attacker can set a known session ID before the victim logs in and then use it to access the authenticated session.
tests.why_it_matters: Session fixation allows attackers to hijack sessions without needing to steal tokens. By setting the session ID before authentication, the attacker guarantees they know the token that will be associated with the victim's authenticated session.
what_to_check: Record the session ID before login, then check if it changes after successful authentication
Test whether session IDs provided in URL parameters are accepted by the server
Check if session IDs can be set through cookie injection techniques
Verify that the old session ID is invalidated after a new one is generated
Test if session fixation is possible through cross-subdomain cookie injection

tests.name: Testing for Exposed Session Variables
tests.description: This test checks whether session tokens or session-related data are exposed through insecure channels such as URLs (Referer header leakage), error messages, logs, or cached pages. Exposed session variables can be captured by third parties or found in server logs.
tests.why_it_matters: Session tokens exposed in URLs are particularly dangerous because they appear in browser history, server logs, proxy logs, and Referer headers sent to third-party sites. Any of these exposure points can lead to session hijacking.
what_to_check: Check if session tokens appear in URLs as query parameters
Verify that session data is not leaked through Referer headers to third-party sites
Test whether error pages or debug output expose session-related information
Check server logs for session token recording
Verify that session data is not exposed through browser history or cached pages

tests.name: Testing for Cross Site Request Forgery
tests.description: Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF) testing checks whether the application protects state-changing operations from being triggered by malicious sites that the user visits while authenticated. CSRF exploits the trust that the server places in the user's browser.
tests.why_it_matters: CSRF attacks can force authenticated users to perform unintended actions like changing their password, transferring funds, or modifying account settings without their knowledge. The attack works because the browser automatically includes session cookies with every request to the target domain.
what_to_check: Check for anti-CSRF tokens in forms that perform state-changing operations
Verify that anti-CSRF tokens are validated server-side and are unique per session
Test if removing or modifying the CSRF token still allows the request to succeed
Check SameSite cookie attribute which provides CSRF protection at the browser level
Test if sensitive actions can be performed via GET requests (which bypass some CSRF protections)

tests.name: Testing for Logout Functionality
tests.description: This test verifies that the logout function properly destroys the session on the server side, clears session cookies on the client side, and that the session token cannot be reused after logout. Incomplete logout leaves sessions vulnerable to reuse.
tests.why_it_matters: If logout does not properly invalidate the session server-side, an attacker who obtained a session token can continue using it even after the legitimate user logs out. This is especially critical on shared computers and in environments where session tokens may have been compromised.
what_to_check: Verify that clicking logout destroys the session on the server (not just client-side)
Test whether the session token can be reused after logout by replaying it
Check that session cookies are properly cleared with appropriate expiration values
Verify that the back button does not display authenticated content after logout
Test if logout invalidates all sessions for the user or only the current one

tests.name: Testing Session Timeout
tests.description: This test verifies that sessions expire after an appropriate period of inactivity (idle timeout) and after a maximum absolute lifetime. Sessions that persist indefinitely increase the window of opportunity for session hijacking.
tests.why_it_matters: Long-lived or non-expiring sessions dramatically increase the risk of session hijacking because they give attackers more time to discover and use stolen session tokens. Appropriate timeouts limit the damage window and force re-authentication.
what_to_check: Test the idle timeout by waiting without activity and then attempting to use the session
Verify that a maximum session lifetime exists regardless of activity
Check if the session timeout is enforced server-side, not just via client-side JavaScript
Test whether session timeout values are appropriate for the sensitivity of the application
Verify that expired sessions cannot be revived by sending requests with the old token

tests.name: Testing for Session Puzzling
tests.description: Session puzzling (session variable overloading) occurs when the application uses the same session variable for different purposes across different parts of the application. An attacker can set a session variable in one context and exploit it in another context to bypass security controls.
tests.why_it_matters: Session puzzling exploits the shared nature of server-side session storage. When different application functions read and write the same session variables, an attacker can manipulate values through one function to influence the behavior of another, potentially bypassing authentication or authorization.
what_to_check: Map all session variables used across different application functions
Identify session variables that are set in one context and read in another
Test whether manipulating session variables through one function affects others
Check if unauthenticated functions set session variables used by authenticated functions
Verify that session variables have well-defined scopes and purposes

tests.name: Testing for Session Hijacking
tests.description: Session hijacking testing evaluates the overall resilience of the session management implementation against various token theft and replay techniques. This includes testing network-level interception, XSS-based theft, and whether additional controls like IP binding or device fingerprinting are in place.
tests.why_it_matters: Session hijacking is the culmination of session management weaknesses. If an attacker can obtain and replay a session token, they gain full access to the victim's account. Comprehensive testing ensures that multiple layers of defense protect against this attack.
what_to_check: Test whether sessions are bound to additional client attributes (IP, User-Agent)
Verify that concurrent session limits or alerts exist for the same account
Check if the application detects and responds to session token replay from different locations
Test whether Man-in-the-Middle attacks can capture session tokens on the network
Verify that all session management protections work together as defense in depth

categories.name: Input Validation Testing
categories.summary: Input Validation Testing covers the broadest and most technically deep category of web security tests, examining how the application handles untrusted input across all attack vectors. From XSS and SQL injection to SSRF and template injection, these vulnerabilities arise when user input is processed without proper validation and sanitization.
categories.intro: Input validation is the most critical defense in web application security. This category contains 19 tests covering every major injection attack class, from cross-site scripting and SQL injection to server-side request forgery and template injection. Each test examines a different way that untrusted user input can be weaponized to compromise the application, its users, or its underlying infrastructure. Input validation vulnerabilities consistently dominate real-world breach reports because they can lead to data theft, code execution, and complete system compromise. For penetration testers and bug bounty hunters, mastering these techniques is essential since injection vulnerabilities typically command the highest severity ratings and payouts. Cert candidates will encounter these concepts heavily in CEH, OSCP, and GWAPT examinations.

faq_items.q: What is the difference between input validation and output encoding?
faq_items.a: Input validation checks that incoming data conforms to expected formats and rejects anything that does not match. Output encoding transforms data so it is safe for the specific context where it is rendered (HTML, SQL, JavaScript, etc.). Both are necessary: input validation reduces the attack surface, while output encoding prevents exploitation even if validation is bypassed. A defense-in-depth approach uses both.

faq_items.q: Why is SQL injection still one of the most common web vulnerabilities?
faq_items.a: SQL injection persists because many applications still construct SQL queries by concatenating user input rather than using parameterized queries. Legacy applications, custom ORM layers, and stored procedures with dynamic SQL all create opportunities for injection. Additionally, complex query logic and developer shortcuts under time pressure continue to introduce new injection points even in modern applications.

faq_items.q: What is Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF) and why has it become more important?
faq_items.a: SSRF occurs when an attacker can make the server send requests to unintended destinations, typically internal services or cloud metadata endpoints. It has become more important with cloud adoption because SSRF can access cloud metadata services (like AWS IMDSv1 at 169.254.169.254) to steal credentials and pivot to other cloud resources. SSRF was added to the OWASP Top 10 in 2021 as A10.

tests.name: Testing for Reflected Cross Site Scripting
tests.description: Reflected XSS testing checks if user input is immediately returned in the server's response without proper encoding. An attacker crafts a malicious URL containing JavaScript that executes in the victim's browser when they click the link, potentially stealing session tokens or performing actions on their behalf.
tests.why_it_matters: Reflected XSS is one of the most common web vulnerabilities and enables session hijacking, credential theft, and phishing attacks. Because the payload is delivered via a URL, it can be spread through phishing emails, social media, or watering hole attacks.
what_to_check: Test all URL parameters with basic XSS payloads (<script>alert(1)</script>)
Check search fields, error messages, and any reflected input for encoding
Test for XSS in different HTML contexts (attribute, JavaScript, CSS, URL)
Try encoding bypass techniques (HTML entities, URL encoding, Unicode)
Verify that Content-Type headers and Content-Security-Policy prevent execution

tests.name: Testing for Stored Cross Site Scripting
tests.description: Stored XSS testing checks if user input is saved by the application (in a database, file, or cache) and later displayed to other users without proper encoding. Unlike reflected XSS, stored XSS does not require victim interaction with a malicious link since the payload is served from the application itself.
tests.why_it_matters: Stored XSS is more dangerous than reflected XSS because the malicious payload persists in the application and automatically executes for every user who views the affected page. A single stored XSS vulnerability in a popular feature can compromise thousands of users.
what_to_check: Test all user-input fields that store and display data (comments, profiles, messages)
Check file upload names and metadata for XSS in filenames displayed on the page
Test rich text editors for XSS through HTML/Markdown input
Verify that stored data is encoded when displayed in all contexts
Check administrative panels that display user-submitted data

tests.name: Testing for HTTP Verb Tampering
tests.description: HTTP verb tampering tests whether changing the HTTP method (GET, POST, PUT, HEAD, etc.) on a request can bypass security controls. Some applications and middleware only enforce authentication or authorization on specific HTTP methods, leaving others unprotected.
tests.why_it_matters: Security controls that only apply to specific HTTP methods can be trivially bypassed. For example, a web application firewall might only inspect POST requests for SQL injection, allowing the same attack through a PUT request. This is a common misconfiguration in access control implementations.
what_to_check: Replay protected requests using different HTTP methods (GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, DELETE)
Test if changing the method bypasses authentication or authorization checks
Check if the application responds differently to unexpected HTTP methods
Test for method override using X-HTTP-Method-Override header
Verify that security controls apply uniformly across all HTTP methods

tests.name: Testing for HTTP Parameter Pollution
tests.description: HTTP Parameter Pollution (HPP) testing checks how the application handles duplicate parameters in the same request. Different web servers and frameworks handle duplicate parameters differently (first value, last value, concatenated, or array), which can be exploited to bypass validation or manipulate application logic.
tests.why_it_matters: HPP can bypass web application firewalls and input validation by splitting malicious input across duplicate parameters that get reassembled on the server. It can also manipulate application logic when different components parse duplicate parameters differently.
what_to_check: Submit duplicate parameters with the same name but different values
Test how the application and server handle duplicate parameters (first wins, last wins, concat)
Try splitting injection payloads across duplicate parameters
Test HPP in query strings, POST body, and cookies simultaneously
Check if WAF rules can be bypassed through parameter pollution

tests.name: Testing for SQL Injection
tests.description: SQL injection testing checks whether user input is incorporated into SQL queries without proper parameterization or sanitization. Successful SQL injection can read, modify, or delete database contents, bypass authentication, and in some cases execute operating system commands.
tests.why_it_matters: SQL injection has been responsible for some of the largest data breaches in history, exposing millions of records. It can lead to complete database compromise, authentication bypass, data exfiltration, and in some configurations, remote code execution on the database server.
what_to_check: Test all input parameters with SQL metacharacters (single quote, double quote, semicolon)
Check for error-based, union-based, blind, and time-based SQL injection techniques
Test numeric, string, and wildcard parameter types
Look for second-order SQL injection where stored input is used in later queries
Test for SQL injection in HTTP headers, cookies, and JSON/XML request bodies

tests.name: Testing for LDAP Injection
tests.description: LDAP injection testing checks whether user input is used in LDAP queries without proper sanitization. LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol) is commonly used for authentication and directory lookups in enterprise environments, and injection can bypass authentication or extract directory data.
tests.why_it_matters: LDAP injection can bypass authentication in enterprise applications that use Active Directory or other LDAP directories. It can also extract sensitive directory information including user lists, email addresses, organizational structures, and group memberships.
what_to_check: Test login and search fields with LDAP metacharacters (*, (, ), \, NUL)
Try authentication bypass using LDAP injection (e.g., user=*)(uid=*)(&password=anything)
Test for information extraction by manipulating LDAP filter expressions
Check if the application uses parameterized LDAP queries or string concatenation
Test for both AND-based and OR-based LDAP injection techniques

tests.name: Testing for XML Injection
tests.description: XML injection testing checks whether user input is embedded in XML documents without proper encoding, allowing attackers to modify XML structure. This includes XML External Entity (XXE) attacks that can read files, perform SSRF, or cause denial of service.
tests.why_it_matters: XML injection and XXE attacks can read arbitrary files from the server, perform server-side request forgery to internal services, exfiltrate data through out-of-band channels, and cause denial of service through billion laughs or recursive entity expansion attacks.
what_to_check: Test for XML External Entity (XXE) injection to read local files
Check for blind XXE using out-of-band data exfiltration techniques
Test XML input for entity expansion denial of service (billion laughs)
Verify that XML parsers have external entity processing disabled
Test SOAP endpoints for XML injection in parameters

tests.name: Testing for SSI Injection
tests.description: Server-Side Includes (SSI) injection testing checks whether user input is processed by the SSI engine, allowing attackers to inject SSI directives that execute commands, include files, or display server information. SSI is a legacy technology still found in some web servers.
tests.why_it_matters: SSI injection can lead to remote code execution if the web server processes SSI directives in user-controlled content. While less common in modern applications, legacy systems and certain hosting configurations still support SSI processing.
what_to_check: Test input fields with SSI directives (<!--#exec cmd="command" -->)
Check if .shtml or .stm file extensions are processed for SSI
Test for SSI injection in file upload names and content
Verify that the web server is configured to only process SSI in designated file types
Check error pages and dynamic content for SSI processing

tests.name: Testing for XPath Injection
tests.description: XPath injection testing checks whether user input is used in XPath queries against XML data stores without proper sanitization. Similar to SQL injection but targeting XML databases, XPath injection can bypass authentication and extract data from XML documents.
tests.why_it_matters: XPath injection can bypass authentication and extract all data from XML-based data stores. Unlike SQL injection, XPath has no permission model, so a successful injection can always access the entire XML document. Applications using XML for configuration or data storage are at risk.
what_to_check: Test input fields with XPath metacharacters (single quote, double quote, //, ..)
Try authentication bypass with tautology-based XPath injection (1=1)
Test for blind XPath injection using boolean conditions and substring extraction
Check if the application uses XML databases or XPath for data retrieval
Verify that XPath queries use parameterized expressions rather than concatenation

tests.name: Testing for IMAP SMTP Injection
tests.description: IMAP/SMTP injection testing checks whether user input is incorporated into email-related commands without sanitization. This can allow attackers to inject email commands through webmail interfaces, contact forms, or password reset functions that interact with mail servers.
tests.why_it_matters: Email injection can allow attackers to send emails as the application (spam, phishing), read other users' emails through IMAP injection, modify mailbox contents, or exfiltrate data through crafted email messages sent to attacker-controlled addresses.
what_to_check: Test contact forms and feedback features for email header injection (\r\n)
Check if user input is used in IMAP commands for webmail applications
Test for SMTP injection through newline characters in email fields
Verify that email addresses are validated before use in mail commands
Test password reset and notification features for email injection

tests.name: Testing for Code Injection
tests.description: Code injection testing checks whether user input is evaluated as code by the application's runtime environment. This includes injection into interpreted languages like PHP, Python, Ruby, and JavaScript where functions like eval(), exec(), or system() process user-supplied data.
tests.why_it_matters: Code injection provides direct code execution on the server, which is typically the most severe class of vulnerability. An attacker who can inject code gains the full capabilities of the programming language, including file system access, network operations, and command execution.
what_to_check: Test parameters for server-side code injection (PHP, Python, Ruby, Node.js)
Look for eval(), exec(), system(), or similar dangerous functions processing user input
Test for code injection through deserialization of untrusted data
Check for expression language injection in framework-specific contexts
Verify that dynamic code evaluation is not used with user-controlled input

tests.name: Testing for Command Injection
tests.description: Command injection (OS command injection) testing checks whether user input is passed to operating system commands without proper sanitization. Successful exploitation allows attackers to execute arbitrary commands on the server with the privileges of the web application.
tests.why_it_matters: Command injection provides direct access to the underlying operating system. Attackers can read files, modify data, install backdoors, pivot to internal networks, or completely take over the server. It is one of the most severe vulnerability classes.
what_to_check: Test parameters with command separator characters (;, |, &&, ||, `, $())
Look for application features that interact with the OS (ping, traceroute, file operations)
Test for blind command injection using time delays (sleep, ping -c)
Try out-of-band data exfiltration through DNS or HTTP callbacks
Check for command injection in file upload names and processing pipelines

tests.name: Testing for Format String Injection
tests.description: Format string injection testing checks whether user input is used as a format string parameter in functions like printf() in C/C++ applications. This can be exploited to read from and write to memory, crash the application, or achieve code execution.
tests.why_it_matters: Format string vulnerabilities can lead to information disclosure, denial of service, and arbitrary code execution. While less common in modern web applications, they still appear in applications with native code components, embedded systems, and legacy C/C++ web services.
what_to_check: Test input parameters with format string specifiers (%s, %x, %n, %p)
Look for applications with C/C++ backends that use printf-family functions
Test for information leakage through %x or %p reading stack memory
Check CGI applications and native code web services for format string issues
Verify that user input is never used as the format string argument

tests.name: Testing for Incubated Vulnerability
tests.description: Incubated vulnerability testing checks for attacks where malicious input is stored in one part of the application and triggered later in a different context.
