This includes second-order injection attacks where stored data is used in SQL queries, file operations, or displayed to other users.
tests.why_it_matters: Incubated vulnerabilities are harder to detect because the injection point and the execution point are separated in time and location within the application. Automated scanners often miss these because they test input and output in the same request, not across different workflows.
what_to_check: Trace how stored user input is used in subsequent application operations
Test for second-order SQL injection in stored data processed by backend jobs
Check if uploaded files or data trigger processing pipelines that may be vulnerable
Look for stored input used in email templates, report generation, or data exports
Test registration data that might be used in administrative interfaces without encoding

tests.name: Testing for HTTP Splitting Smuggling
tests.description: HTTP splitting and smuggling testing checks whether user input can inject headers or manipulate request boundaries in HTTP traffic. HTTP request smuggling exploits differences in how front-end and back-end servers parse HTTP requests to bypass security controls.
tests.why_it_matters: HTTP request smuggling can bypass web application firewalls, access controls, and routing rules by causing front-end and back-end servers to disagree about request boundaries. It can lead to cache poisoning, session hijacking, and request routing manipulation.
what_to_check: Test for HTTP response splitting by injecting CRLF characters in parameters
Check for HTTP request smuggling using Content-Length/Transfer-Encoding conflicts
Test for CL.TE and TE.CL smuggling variants
Verify that the application rejects requests with ambiguous Content-Length/Transfer-Encoding
Check if user input can inject HTTP headers through CRLF injection

tests.name: Testing for HTTP Incoming Requests
tests.description: This test examines how the application handles and validates incoming HTTP requests, including unusual or malformed requests. It checks whether the application properly validates request structure, encoding, and content before processing.
tests.why_it_matters: Applications that do not properly validate incoming HTTP requests may be vulnerable to request manipulation, encoding-based bypasses, or protocol-level attacks. Strict request validation is an important defense-in-depth measure.
what_to_check: Test how the application handles malformed HTTP requests
Check processing of requests with unusual encodings (UTF-7, double URL encoding)
Verify that the application validates Content-Type headers match actual content
Test for request body processing differences between content types
Check how the application handles oversized requests and headers

tests.name: Testing for Host Header Injection
tests.description: Host header injection testing checks whether the application trusts the Host header from user requests for security-sensitive operations like password reset links, cache keys, or server-side redirects. An attacker can manipulate the Host header to poison web caches or steal password reset tokens.
tests.why_it_matters: Host header injection can lead to password reset poisoning (where reset links point to attacker-controlled domains), web cache poisoning, and server-side request routing manipulation. Applications that use the Host header to generate URLs are particularly vulnerable.
what_to_check: Modify the Host header in password reset requests to an attacker-controlled domain
Test for web cache poisoning by changing the Host header and checking cached responses
Check for SSRF through absolute URL handling with a modified Host header
Test X-Forwarded-Host and X-Host headers that may override the original Host
Verify that the application validates the Host header against a whitelist

tests.name: Testing for Server-side Template Injection
tests.description: Server-Side Template Injection (SSTI) testing checks whether user input is embedded in server-side templates (Jinja2, Twig, Freemarker, etc.) without proper sanitization. SSTI can escalate from information disclosure to remote code execution depending on the template engine.
tests.why_it_matters: SSTI vulnerabilities are especially dangerous because template engines often have access to server-side objects and methods. Depending on the template engine, SSTI can be escalated from simple math expressions to reading files, executing commands, and achieving full server compromise.
what_to_check: Test input fields with template syntax for common engines ({{7*7}}, ${7*7}, #{7*7})
Identify the template engine from error messages or expression evaluation
Test for code execution through template engine-specific object chains
Check if user input is rendered in templates for emails, reports, or dynamic pages
Verify that user input is always placed in template data context, never in template code

tests.name: Testing for Server-Side Request Forgery
tests.description: Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF) testing checks whether the application can be tricked into making HTTP requests to unintended destinations, particularly internal services. SSRF exploits server-side functionality that fetches URLs, imports data, or processes webhooks to access internal resources.
tests.why_it_matters: SSRF is a critical vulnerability in cloud environments because it can access cloud metadata services to steal IAM credentials, interact with internal services not exposed to the internet, and pivot through the internal network. It was elevated to the OWASP Top 10 in 2021 as A10.
what_to_check: Test URL input parameters with internal IP addresses (127.0.0.1, 169.254.169.254, 10.x.x.x)
Check for cloud metadata endpoint access (AWS, GCP, Azure metadata services)
Test for SSRF through file import, URL preview, webhook, and PDF generation features
Try bypass techniques for SSRF filters (DNS rebinding, URL encoding, IPv6, redirects)
Test for blind SSRF using out-of-band callbacks to attacker-controlled servers

categories.name: Error Handling
categories.summary: Error Handling testing examines how the application responds when things go wrong. Improper error handling can leak sensitive information about the application's internal structure, technology stack, and database through detailed error messages and stack traces.
categories.intro: Error handling is a deceptively simple concept that has significant security implications. When applications encounter errors, they must balance providing useful feedback to users while not revealing internal details to attackers. This category tests whether error responses expose stack traces, database queries, file paths, server versions, or other information that helps attackers understand and exploit the application. Proper error handling returns generic user-friendly messages while logging detailed errors server-side. For security testers, triggering errors is a valuable reconnaissance technique that often reveals more about the application than any other single test. This is one of the most beginner-friendly testing categories and an excellent starting point for those new to web security testing.

faq_items.q: Why is error handling important for web application security?
faq_items.a: Error handling is important because detailed error messages can reveal sensitive internal information to attackers, including database schemas, file paths, technology versions, and internal logic. This information helps attackers craft targeted exploits. Proper error handling shows generic messages to users while logging technical details server-side for debugging.

faq_items.q: What kind of information can stack traces expose?
faq_items.a: Stack traces can expose programming language and version, framework and library versions, internal file paths and directory structures, database connection strings and query details, internal function and variable names, and sometimes even source code snippets. Each piece of information helps an attacker build a more targeted attack against the application.

faq_items.q: How should web applications handle errors securely?
faq_items.a: Applications should return generic, user-friendly error pages (like a simple 'Something went wrong') without technical details. All detailed error information should be logged server-side with correlation IDs that support staff can use to look up the details. Applications should never display stack traces, SQL queries, or internal paths to end users, even in development environments exposed to the internet.

tests.name: Testing for Improper Error Handling
tests.description: This test triggers error conditions in the application and examines the error responses for sensitive information leakage. It covers HTTP error codes, application error messages, and edge cases that produce unhandled exceptions revealing internal details.
tests.why_it_matters: Improper error handling is one of the easiest vulnerabilities to find and one of the most informative for attackers. A single verbose error message can reveal the database type, query structure, internal file paths, and application framework, saving the attacker hours of reconnaissance.
what_to_check: Trigger 404 errors and check if default server error pages reveal version information
Submit invalid input to form fields and check for database error messages
Send malformed requests (wrong Content-Type, missing parameters) and analyze responses
Test for different error behavior between authenticated and unauthenticated states
Check if error messages differ in a way that reveals valid versus invalid resources

tests.name: Testing for Stack Traces
tests.description: This test specifically targets the exposure of stack traces in error responses. Stack traces provide a detailed call chain showing exactly which functions were executed, the file paths, line numbers, and often the parameters that caused the error.
tests.why_it_matters: Stack traces are a goldmine for attackers because they reveal the exact technology stack, internal code structure, third-party libraries with versions, and the precise logic that failed. This information directly informs exploitation strategies and helps identify specific CVEs to target.
what_to_check: Submit unexpected data types to trigger type conversion errors
Send oversized input to trigger buffer or memory-related errors
Request non-existent resources to trigger routing or file system errors
Test API endpoints with malformed JSON or XML to trigger parsing errors
Check if stack traces are returned in HTTP response body, headers, or both

categories.name: Cryptography
categories.summary: Cryptography testing evaluates how the application implements encryption, hashing, and secure communication protocols. Weak or misconfigured cryptography can expose sensitive data in transit and at rest, undermining the confidentiality that users and regulations demand.
categories.intro: Cryptography is the backbone of data protection in web applications, securing everything from login credentials to payment information. This category tests whether the application uses strong, properly configured encryption for data in transit (TLS/SSL) and at rest, and whether cryptographic implementations are free from known weaknesses like padding oracle attacks. Testing cryptography requires understanding both the protocols in use and the common implementation mistakes that weaken them. Weak TLS configurations, deprecated cipher suites, and improper certificate validation are among the most commonly found issues. These vulnerabilities directly impact compliance with standards like PCI DSS, HIPAA, and GDPR, making cryptography testing essential for both security assessments and regulatory audits.

faq_items.q: What are the most common cryptographic weaknesses in web applications?
faq_items.a: The most common weaknesses include using deprecated TLS versions (TLS 1.0, 1.1, SSL 3.0), weak cipher suites (RC4, DES, export ciphers), missing HSTS headers, improper certificate validation, transmitting sensitive data over HTTP, and using weak hashing algorithms (MD5, SHA-1) for passwords instead of purpose-built password hashing functions like bcrypt or Argon2.

faq_items.q: What is a padding oracle attack and how does it work?
faq_items.a: A padding oracle attack exploits applications that reveal whether the padding of an encrypted message is valid through different error messages or response times. By systematically modifying ciphertext bytes and observing the application's response, an attacker can decrypt the entire message without knowing the key. Prevention requires using authenticated encryption (like AES-GCM) and ensuring consistent error responses.

faq_items.q: How does cryptography testing relate to compliance requirements like PCI DSS?
faq_items.a: PCI DSS mandates strong cryptography for transmitting and storing cardholder data, requiring TLS 1.2 or higher, prohibiting weak ciphers, and mandating proper key management. HIPAA requires encryption of electronic protected health information, and GDPR expects encryption as a technical measure for data protection. Failing cryptography tests often means failing compliance audits as well.

tests.name: Testing for Weak Transport Layer Security
tests.description: This test evaluates the TLS/SSL configuration of the application including supported protocol versions, cipher suites, certificate validity, and key exchange mechanisms. Weak transport security allows attackers to intercept, decrypt, or modify data in transit.
tests.why_it_matters: Transport layer security is the primary protection for data in transit between users and the application. Weak TLS configurations expose sensitive data to network-level attacks including eavesdropping, man-in-the-middle attacks, and protocol downgrade attacks like POODLE and BEAST.
what_to_check: Verify that only TLS 1.2 and TLS 1.3 are supported (no SSL 3.0, TLS 1.0, or TLS 1.1)
Check for weak cipher suites (RC4, DES, 3DES, export ciphers, NULL ciphers)
Validate certificate chain, expiration, and revocation status
Test for known TLS vulnerabilities (POODLE, BEAST, Heartbleed, CRIME, BREACH)
Verify that perfect forward secrecy (PFS) cipher suites are preferred

tests.name: Testing for Padding Oracle
tests.description: This test checks whether the application is vulnerable to padding oracle attacks by analyzing how it responds to modified ciphertext. If the application reveals whether decryption padding is valid (through error messages or timing differences), attackers can decrypt ciphertext without the key.
tests.why_it_matters: Padding oracle attacks can decrypt any data encrypted with CBC-mode block ciphers when the application leaks padding validation results. This has been exploited in real-world attacks against ASP.NET, Java applications, and custom encryption implementations to steal session tokens, decrypt cookies, and forge encrypted data.
what_to_check: Identify encrypted values in cookies, URL parameters, or hidden form fields
Modify encrypted values and observe error response differences
Measure response time differences between valid and invalid padding
Test encrypted session tokens for padding oracle vulnerabilities
Check if the application uses CBC mode without authenticated encryption

tests.name: Testing for Sensitive Information Sent via Unencrypted Channels
tests.description: This test checks whether sensitive data such as credentials, personal information, payment details, or session tokens are transmitted over unencrypted HTTP connections. Even applications with HTTPS may have pages or API endpoints that fall back to HTTP.
tests.why_it_matters: Sensitive data transmitted over HTTP is visible to anyone who can observe network traffic, including users on the same Wi-Fi network, ISPs, and anyone with access to network infrastructure. This is trivially exploitable with freely available tools like Wireshark.
what_to_check: Verify that all pages handling sensitive data are served exclusively over HTTPS
Check for mixed content where HTTPS pages load resources over HTTP
Test whether login forms can be accessed and submitted over HTTP
Verify that API endpoints transmitting sensitive data require HTTPS
Check for HTTP links in emails sent by the application (password reset, verification)

tests.name: Testing for Weak Encryption
tests.description: This test evaluates whether the application uses strong, current encryption algorithms and proper key management for data at rest. Weak encryption includes deprecated algorithms (DES, RC4, MD5), insufficient key lengths, hardcoded keys, and improper use of cryptographic primitives.
tests.why_it_matters: Weak encryption provides a false sense of security. Data encrypted with broken algorithms or short keys can be decrypted by attackers with moderate resources. Hardcoded encryption keys found in source code or configuration files compromise all data encrypted with those keys.
what_to_check: Identify encryption algorithms used for data at rest (passwords, PII, payment data)
Verify that passwords use adaptive hashing algorithms (bcrypt, Argon2, scrypt) not MD5/SHA-1
Check for hardcoded encryption keys in source code or configuration files
Verify that key lengths meet current recommendations (AES-256, RSA-2048 minimum)
Test for use of ECB mode which leaks data patterns in encrypted output

categories.name: Business Logic Testing
categories.summary: Business Logic Testing examines the application's workflow rules and business processes for flaws that allow attackers to manipulate intended functionality. Unlike technical vulnerabilities, logic flaws exploit legitimate features in unintended ways and are invisible to automated scanners.
categories.intro: Business logic vulnerabilities are among the most challenging to find and the most impactful when exploited. Unlike injection or configuration flaws that follow known patterns, business logic bugs are unique to each application because they exploit the specific rules and workflows the application implements. This category tests whether business processes can be bypassed, manipulated, or abused, covering everything from data validation and request forgery to file upload restrictions and rate limits. Automated security scanners cannot detect these vulnerabilities because they require understanding the application's intended behavior and testing what happens when that behavior is subverted. For penetration testers and bug bounty hunters, business logic testing is where deep application knowledge pays off with high-severity findings that others miss.

faq_items.q: What are business logic vulnerabilities and why are they hard to find?
faq_items.a: Business logic vulnerabilities are flaws in the application's intended workflow that allow users to achieve outcomes the developers did not anticipate. They are hard to find because they require understanding the application's business rules and testing edge cases that automated scanners cannot identify. Examples include skipping required steps in a checkout process, manipulating quantities to get negative prices, or reusing single-use coupon codes.

faq_items.q: Can automated scanners detect business logic flaws?
faq_items.a: No, automated scanners generally cannot detect business logic flaws because these vulnerabilities require understanding the intended behavior of the application. A scanner cannot know that skipping step 3 of a 5-step process should not be allowed, or that a coupon should only work once. Manual testing by someone who understands the application's business rules is essential for finding these issues.

faq_items.q: What is the relationship between business logic testing and threat modeling?
faq_items.a: Threat modeling identifies potential business logic abuse scenarios before testing begins by analyzing how the application's features and workflows could be manipulated. It asks questions like 'What if a user submits step 4 before step 2?' or 'What if the quantity is negative?' This systematic approach ensures business logic testing is comprehensive rather than relying on ad-hoc exploration.

tests.name: Test Business Logic Data Validation
tests.description: This test checks whether the application properly validates data at the business logic level, beyond basic input validation. It examines whether business rules about data ranges, relationships between fields, and context-specific constraints are properly enforced.
tests.why_it_matters: Business logic validation failures allow attackers to submit data that passes technical validation but violates business rules. For example, ordering a negative quantity, setting a future birth date, or submitting a transaction amount that exceeds account limits may all pass input validation but violate business logic.
what_to_check: Test boundary values for business-specific fields (quantities, amounts, dates)
Submit negative values where only positive values should be accepted
Test relationships between dependent fields (start date after end date, amounts exceeding limits)
Verify that server-side business rules match client-side validation
Check if business constraints are enforced consistently across all entry points

tests.name: Test Ability to Forge Requests
tests.description: This test checks whether the application relies on client-side data or hidden form fields for business logic decisions that should be enforced server-side. Attackers who can forge or modify requests can manipulate prices, skip validation steps, or change transaction parameters.
tests.why_it_matters: Applications that trust client-submitted data for business decisions are vulnerable to request forgery. An attacker can modify prices in hidden form fields, change product IDs, alter quantity values, or manipulate discount codes by intercepting and modifying HTTP requests.
what_to_check: Modify hidden form fields that control pricing or business logic
Change product IDs or SKUs in purchase requests to get different items at wrong prices
Manipulate request parameters that control workflow steps or approval states
Test if removing or modifying referrer checks allows skipping validation steps
Verify that all business-critical values are validated server-side, not just client-side

tests.name: Test Integrity Checks
tests.description: This test verifies whether the application implements integrity checks to detect unauthorized modification of data in transit or at rest. It checks for mechanisms like checksums, digital signatures, and tamper-evident logging that protect against data manipulation.
tests.why_it_matters: Without integrity checks, attackers can modify data without detection. This includes changing transaction amounts, altering audit logs, modifying configuration files, or tampering with data exchanged between application components. Integrity verification is a core requirement for financial and regulated applications.
what_to_check: Check if the application uses checksums or HMACs to verify data integrity
Test whether modifying data in transit (cookies, hidden fields, API payloads) is detected
Verify that audit logs are tamper-evident and modifications are detectable
Test if transaction integrity is maintained across multi-step processes
Check whether data integrity verification covers all critical data paths

tests.name: Test for Process Timing
tests.description: This test checks whether the application is vulnerable to timing-based attacks on business processes. This includes race conditions where concurrent requests can bypass limits, time-of-check-to-time-of-use (TOCTOU) bugs, and timing-based information leakage.
tests.why_it_matters: Race conditions and timing attacks can bypass business rules that work correctly for sequential requests. For example, simultaneously submitting two withdrawal requests might allow double-spending if the balance check and deduction are not atomic. These bugs often appear in financial, inventory, and coupon systems.
what_to_check: Test for race conditions by sending concurrent requests for the same operation
Check if time-sensitive operations (OTP validation, coupon redemption) are vulnerable to replay
Test for TOCTOU vulnerabilities in multi-step business processes
Measure response time differences that might leak information about internal state
Verify that rate-limiting and balance checks are atomic operations

tests.name: Test Number of Times a Function Can Be Used Limits
tests.description: This test checks whether the application enforces limits on how many times specific functions can be used. This includes rate limiting, usage quotas, one-time-use features (coupons, vouchers, trials), and maximum attempt limits.
tests.why_it_matters: Without proper usage limits, attackers can abuse features far beyond their intended use. Single-use coupons can be reused, free trial periods can be restarted indefinitely, and unlimited API calls can be used for data scraping or denial of service.
what_to_check: Test if single-use features (coupons, voucher codes, trial activations) can be reused
Check if rate limiting exists on sensitive operations (login attempts, API calls, downloads)
Verify that rate limits are enforced server-side and cannot be bypassed
Test if changing session identifiers, IP addresses, or accounts bypasses usage limits
Check if free trial limits can be circumvented through account creation

tests.name: Testing for the Circumvention of Work Flows
tests.description: This test checks whether the application enforces the intended sequence of steps in multi-step processes. Attackers may attempt to skip, reorder, or repeat steps in workflows like registration, checkout, or approval processes to achieve unintended outcomes.
tests.why_it_matters: Workflow circumvention can allow attackers to bypass critical security or business steps. Skipping payment verification in a checkout process, bypassing email verification in registration, or jumping directly to the approval step in a review process can have severe business consequences.
what_to_check: Test if steps in multi-step processes can be skipped by directly requesting later steps
Check if workflow steps can be completed out of order
Verify that completing a workflow step requires all previous steps to be genuinely completed
Test if repeating earlier steps after completing later steps causes inconsistencies
Check whether canceling and restarting workflows preserves or resets state correctly

tests.name: Test Defenses Against Application Misuse
tests.description: This test evaluates whether the application has defenses against abuse of legitimate functionality. This includes detecting and blocking automated bot activity, identifying suspicious usage patterns, and implementing safeguards against feature abuse at scale.
tests.why_it_matters: Applications without abuse detection allow attackers to exploit legitimate features for malicious purposes. Scraping user data, creating fake accounts at scale, manipulating review systems, or exploiting referral programs are all examples of application misuse that require proactive detection and prevention.
what_to_check: Test if automated interactions are detected and blocked (CAPTCHAs, bot detection)
Check if suspicious usage patterns trigger alerts or account restrictions
Verify that abuse-prevention measures cannot be easily bypassed
Test if the application logs and monitors for abnormal usage patterns
Check if there are mechanisms to recover from detected abuse scenarios

tests.name: Test Upload of Unexpected File Types
tests.description: This test checks whether the application properly restricts file upload functionality to only accept intended file types. Accepting unexpected file types can lead to web shell uploads, stored XSS through HTML/SVG files, or server-side processing vulnerabilities through malicious documents.
tests.why_it_matters: Unrestricted file upload is a severe vulnerability that can lead to remote code execution if an attacker uploads a web shell, cross-site scripting if HTML or SVG files are served from the same domain, or server compromise through malicious files that exploit document processing libraries.
what_to_check: Test if the application validates file types by extension, MIME type, and file content (magic bytes)
Try uploading executable files with changed extensions (.php.jpg, .asp;.jpg)
Test if MIME type validation can be bypassed by modifying Content-Type headers
Upload HTML and SVG files that contain JavaScript to test for stored XSS
Check if uploaded files are served from a separate domain to prevent same-origin attacks

tests.name: Test Upload of Malicious Files
tests.description: This test goes beyond file type restrictions to check whether the application is vulnerable to malicious content within uploaded files. This includes web shells, documents with embedded macros, polyglot files, and specially crafted files that exploit processing vulnerabilities.
tests.why_it_matters: Even when file type restrictions are in place, malicious content within allowed file types can compromise the system. ZIP bombs can cause denial of service, images with embedded PHP can become web shells on misconfigured servers, and office documents with macros can compromise users who download them.
what_to_check: Upload web shells disguised as allowed file types (PHP in JPEG, JSP in PDF)
Test for ZIP bomb or decompression bomb handling
Upload files with embedded macros or active content
Test for file processing vulnerabilities (ImageTragick, LibreOffice CVEs)
Check if antivirus scanning is performed on uploaded files before they are accessible

categories.name: Client-side Testing
categories.summary: Client-side Testing examines vulnerabilities that execute in the user's browser rather than on the server. This category covers DOM-based XSS, client-side injection, clickjacking, and issues with browser storage, WebSockets, and cross-origin resource sharing.
categories.intro: Client-side vulnerabilities execute in the context of the user's browser and exploit the trust relationship between the browser and the web application. As modern applications shift more logic to the client side through JavaScript frameworks and single-page applications, the attack surface on the client has grown significantly. This category covers 13 tests ranging from DOM-based cross-site scripting and HTML injection to clickjacking, WebSocket testing, and browser storage security. These vulnerabilities are particularly relevant for modern JavaScript-heavy applications where client-side routing, template rendering, and data processing create new attack vectors that traditional server-side testing misses. For security testers, understanding the browser security model and same-origin policy is essential for finding these client-side vulnerabilities.

faq_items.q: What is the difference between reflected/stored XSS and DOM-based XSS?
faq_items.a: Reflected and stored XSS involve the server including malicious input in the HTTP response. DOM-based XSS occurs entirely in the browser when client-side JavaScript reads data from an attacker-controllable source (like the URL fragment) and writes it to a dangerous sink (like innerHTML) without sanitization. DOM-based XSS never reaches the server, making it invisible to server-side security controls and WAFs.

faq_items.q: What is clickjacking and how does it affect users?
faq_items.a: Clickjacking (UI redressing) tricks users into clicking on something different from what they perceive by loading the target application in a transparent iframe overlaid on a malicious page. The user thinks they are clicking buttons on the attacker's page but are actually interacting with the hidden target application. This can lead to unintended actions like changing settings, making purchases, or granting permissions.

faq_items.q: Why is browser storage (localStorage, sessionStorage) a security concern?
faq_items.a: Browser storage is accessible to any JavaScript running on the same origin, which means XSS vulnerabilities can read all stored data. Storing sensitive data like session tokens or personal information in localStorage is risky because it persists even after the browser is closed and has no equivalent of cookie security flags (HttpOnly, Secure). Sensitive data should use httpOnly cookies rather than browser storage.

tests.name: Testing for DOM-Based Cross Site Scripting
tests.description: DOM-based XSS testing checks whether client-side JavaScript reads data from attacker-controllable sources (URL hash, document.referrer, postMessage) and writes it to dangerous sinks (innerHTML, eval, document.write) without proper sanitization. This type of XSS occurs entirely in the browser.
tests.why_it_matters: DOM-based XSS is increasingly common in modern single-page applications and is invisible to server-side security controls because the payload never leaves the browser. Web application firewalls cannot detect or block DOM-based XSS, making client-side testing essential.
what_to_check: Identify JavaScript sources that read from URL fragments, query parameters, or document properties
Map dangerous sinks in JavaScript (innerHTML, eval, document.write, jQuery.html())
Test if data flows from sources to sinks without sanitization
Check for DOM XSS in client-side routing and template rendering
Test postMessage handlers for origin validation and safe data handling

tests.name: Testing for JavaScript Execution
tests.description: This test checks whether an attacker can inject and execute JavaScript through the application's client-side processing. It focuses on contexts where user input is processed by JavaScript code and may be executed as code rather than treated as data.
tests.why_it_matters: JavaScript execution vulnerabilities allow attackers to run arbitrary code in the victim's browser context, enabling session hijacking, data theft, keylogging, and actions on behalf of the user. As applications rely more on client-side JavaScript, these attack vectors expand.
what_to_check: Test for JavaScript injection through URL parameters processed by client-side code
Check for use of eval(), Function(), setTimeout/setInterval with string arguments
Test for injection through JavaScript template literals and string interpolation
Verify that user-controlled data is not used in dynamically generated script content
Check for unsafe JavaScript URL handling (javascript: protocol in links)

tests.name: Testing for HTML Injection
tests.description: HTML injection testing checks whether user input can be used to inject arbitrary HTML content into the page. While similar to XSS, HTML injection focuses on modifying page structure and content (defacement, phishing forms) even when script execution is prevented by Content Security Policy.
tests.why_it_matters: HTML injection can be used for phishing attacks by injecting fake login forms, for content spoofing to damage reputation, and for social engineering by modifying page content. Even when CSP blocks script execution, injected HTML can still manipulate what users see and interact with.
what_to_check: Test input fields for HTML tag injection that modifies page structure
Inject fake login forms to test phishing potential
Check if HTML injection can override or hide legitimate page content
Test for HTML injection in error messages, search results, and user profiles
Verify that Content-Security-Policy prevents escalation to script execution

tests.name: Testing for Client-side URL Redirect
tests.description: This test checks whether the application performs client-side redirects based on user-controllable input without proper validation. Open redirects can be used in phishing attacks by making malicious URLs appear to originate from the trusted application domain.
tests.why_it_matters: Open redirects allow attackers to create convincing phishing URLs using the trusted application's domain. Victims see the legitimate domain in the link and trust it, but are redirected to a malicious site. This can also be chained with other vulnerabilities like OAuth token theft.
what_to_check: Test URL parameters used for redirects (redirect, url, next, return, goto)
Check if JavaScript-based redirects validate the destination URL
Test for redirect bypass techniques (protocol-relative URLs, URL encoding, backslash)
Verify that redirect destinations are validated against a whitelist
Test for open redirect in OAuth flows and login return URLs

tests.name: Testing for CSS Injection
tests.description: CSS injection testing checks whether user input can be injected into style contexts to manipulate page appearance, exfiltrate data through CSS selectors, or in older browsers, execute JavaScript through CSS expression() functions.
tests.why_it_matters: CSS injection can be used to exfiltrate sensitive data (like CSRF tokens) through attribute selectors and background URL techniques, phish users by overlaying fake content, and in some cases execute JavaScript. It is often overlooked because it seems less dangerous than XSS.
what_to_check: Test if user input is reflected in style attributes or CSS blocks
Try injecting CSS that modifies page layout to overlay phishing content
Test CSS-based data exfiltration using attribute selectors and external URL references
Check for CSS injection in user-customizable themes or profile styling
Verify that user input in style contexts is properly sanitized

tests.name: Testing for Client-side Resource Manipulation
tests.description: This test checks whether an attacker can manipulate the resources loaded by the client-side application, such as JavaScript files, CSS stylesheets, images, or iframes. This includes testing for subdomain takeover of CDN resources and manipulation of resource URLs through user input.
tests.why_it_matters: If an attacker can control which resources the application loads, they can inject malicious JavaScript through compromised or attacker-controlled resources. This is particularly relevant when applications load resources from user-controlled URLs or domains vulnerable to takeover.
what_to_check: Identify user-controlled parameters that influence loaded resource URLs
Test if script, stylesheet, or iframe sources can be manipulated through URL parameters
Check for Subresource Integrity (SRI) on externally loaded scripts and stylesheets
Verify that CDN or third-party resource domains are not vulnerable to takeover
Test if the application validates resource origins before loading them

tests.name: Testing Cross Origin Resource Sharing
tests.description: Cross-Origin Resource Sharing (CORS) testing evaluates whether the application's CORS policy allows unauthorized cross-origin access to sensitive data. Overly permissive CORS configurations can allow any website to read authenticated responses from the application's API.
tests.why_it_matters: Misconfigured CORS can allow an attacker's website to make authenticated requests to the target application and read the responses, effectively bypassing the same-origin policy. This can expose user data, tokens, and sensitive API responses to malicious third-party sites.
what_to_check: Test if Access-Control-Allow-Origin reflects the requesting origin without validation
Check if Access-Control-Allow-Credentials is true with a wildcard or reflected origin
Test for origin validation bypasses (null origin, subdomain matching, regex flaws)
Verify that CORS is not configured on endpoints that return sensitive data
Test pre-flight request handling for proper origin validation

tests.name: Testing for Cross Site Flashing
tests.description: Cross-site flashing testing checks whether Flash applications (SWF files) on the server can be exploited to execute JavaScript, perform cross-domain data theft, or redirect users. While Flash is largely deprecated, legacy SWF files may still be hosted and exploitable.
tests.why_it_matters: Legacy Flash files left on servers can still be exploited even though Flash Player is no longer supported by major browsers. Vulnerable SWF files can be used through specialized browser plugins or targeted environments where Flash is still available, and their mere presence indicates poor asset management.
what_to_check: Search for SWF files hosted on the application server
Decompile discovered SWF files and review for user-controlled input handling
Test for FlashVars injection that can alter SWF behavior
Check if SWF files use ExternalInterface for JavaScript communication
Verify that any remaining SWF files are necessary and not exploitable

tests.name: Testing for Clickjacking
tests.description: Clickjacking testing checks whether the application can be loaded in a frame or iframe by a malicious site. If so, an attacker can overlay the application with transparent layers to trick users into clicking on actions they did not intend, such as changing settings or making purchases.
tests.why_it_matters: Clickjacking can trick users into performing any action available in the application including changing account settings, making purchases, transferring funds, or granting permissions. Users believe they are interacting with the attacker's page but are actually clicking on the hidden target application.
what_to_check: Check for X-Frame-Options header (DENY or SAMEORIGIN)
Verify Content-Security-Policy frame-ancestors directive
Test if the application can be loaded in an iframe from a different domain
Check for JavaScript frame-busting code and test if it can be bypassed
Create a proof-of-concept clickjacking page to demonstrate the vulnerability

tests.name: Testing WebSockets
tests.description: WebSocket testing evaluates the security of WebSocket connections used for real-time communication. This includes checking authentication, authorization, input validation, and origin validation on WebSocket endpoints, which often lack the security controls applied to HTTP endpoints.
tests.why_it_matters: WebSockets maintain persistent connections and often bypass HTTP security controls including WAFs, CSRF protections, and rate limiters. Since WebSocket messages are not bound by the same-origin policy after the initial handshake, insecure implementations can be exploited for cross-site attacks.
what_to_check: Check if WebSocket connections validate the Origin header during the handshake
Test if authentication is required and verified for WebSocket connections
Verify that authorization is enforced on WebSocket messages, not just connection establishment
Test WebSocket message content for injection vulnerabilities (XSS, SQL injection)
Check if the WebSocket connection uses the secure wss:// protocol

tests.name: Testing Web Messaging
tests.description: Web messaging testing checks the security of the postMessage API used for cross-origin communication between windows and iframes. Insecure implementations may accept messages from any origin or send sensitive data without verifying the recipient's origin.
tests.why_it_matters: The postMessage API allows cross-origin communication by design, making it a powerful but dangerous feature. If event listeners do not validate message origins, any website can send messages to the application. If the application sends sensitive data via postMessage without checking the target origin, data can be stolen.
what_to_check: Identify all postMessage event listeners in the application's JavaScript
Check if event listeners validate the origin of incoming messages
Test if sending crafted messages from a different origin triggers application functionality
Verify that postMessage calls specify the target origin (not '*')
Check if postMessage data is used in dangerous sinks without sanitization

tests.name: Testing Browser Storage
tests.description: Browser storage testing examines what data the application stores in localStorage, sessionStorage, IndexedDB, and cookies. Sensitive data stored in browser storage is accessible to any JavaScript running on the same origin and may persist longer than intended.
tests.why_it_matters: Data in browser storage is accessible to any JavaScript on the same origin, making it vulnerable to XSS attacks. localStorage persists indefinitely unless explicitly cleared, meaning sensitive data like tokens or personal information can be extracted long after the user session ends.
what_to_check: Review localStorage and sessionStorage for sensitive data (tokens, PII, credentials)
Check if session tokens are stored in browser storage instead of httpOnly cookies
Verify that sensitive data is cleared when the user logs out
Check IndexedDB for cached sensitive data from API responses
Test if stored data is encrypted or if plaintext sensitive values are accessible

tests.name: Testing for Cross Site Script Inclusion
tests.description: Cross-Site Script Inclusion (XSSI) testing checks whether sensitive data is exposed through JavaScript files or JSONP endpoints that can be included by an attacker's page. If authenticated JavaScript responses contain user-specific data, an attacker's page can read this data by including the script.
tests.why_it_matters: XSSI allows an attacker's website to steal sensitive data by including authenticated JavaScript endpoints as script sources. Because script inclusion is not restricted by the same-origin policy, the attacker's page can read any data embedded in the JavaScript response, including CSRF tokens, user data, and API keys.
what_to_check: Identify JavaScript endpoints that return different content based on authentication
Check for JSONP endpoints that include sensitive user data in the callback
Test if authenticated JavaScript responses contain user-specific information
Verify that Content-Type headers prevent script inclusion of non-JavaScript responses
Check if the X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff header prevents MIME type sniffing

categories.name: API Testing
categories.summary: API Testing evaluates the security of application programming interfaces including GraphQL, REST, SOAP, and WebSocket APIs. As APIs become the primary communication mechanism for modern applications, securing them is critical to preventing data breaches and unauthorized access.
categories.intro: APIs are the backbone of modern web applications, mobile apps, and microservice architectures. This category tests the security of different API technologies, each with unique attack surfaces and common vulnerabilities. GraphQL APIs face introspection abuse and query complexity attacks, REST APIs are vulnerable to broken authentication and mass assignment, SOAP APIs have XML-specific injection risks, and WebSocket APIs often lack the security controls applied to traditional HTTP endpoints. As organizations adopt API-first architectures, the attack surface shifts from traditional web pages to API endpoints, making API security testing essential. These tests are increasingly important for security certifications and real-world penetration testing engagements.

faq_items.q: What are the main security differences between REST and GraphQL APIs?
faq_items.a: REST APIs use fixed endpoints with defined response structures, while GraphQL uses a single endpoint where clients specify exactly what data they want. GraphQL introduces unique risks including introspection queries that reveal the entire API schema, deeply nested queries that cause denial of service, and batch query attacks. REST APIs are more susceptible to broken object-level authorization (IDOR) and mass assignment. Both require strong authentication, authorization, and input validation.

faq_items.q: Why are APIs often less secure than web applications?
faq_items.a: APIs are often less secure because they are designed for machine-to-machine communication and may skip security controls that web applications include by default, such as CSRF protection, rate limiting, and WAF coverage. APIs also frequently lack the same level of authentication and authorization enforcement as web interfaces, especially internal APIs that were not designed for public exposure.

faq_items.q: What is the OWASP API Security Top 10 and how does it relate to WSTG?
faq_items.a: The OWASP API Security Top 10 is a separate project focused specifically on API security risks, including Broken Object Level Authorization, Broken Authentication, Excessive Data Exposure, and Injection. WSTG-APIT tests complement this by providing practical testing procedures for different API technologies. Together, they provide comprehensive coverage for API security assessment.

tests.name: Testing GraphQL
tests.description: GraphQL testing evaluates the security of GraphQL API implementations including introspection exposure, query depth and complexity limits, authentication and authorization on resolvers, and injection vulnerabilities in query variables. GraphQL's flexibility creates unique attack surfaces not found in traditional REST APIs.
tests.why_it_matters: GraphQL APIs often expose introspection queries that reveal the entire schema to attackers, allow deeply nested queries that cause denial of service, and may lack authorization checks on individual resolvers. The single endpoint design means traditional WAF rules and rate limiting are harder to apply effectively.
what_to_check: Test if introspection queries are enabled and expose the full API schema
Check for query depth and complexity limits that prevent denial of service
Test authorization enforcement on individual query and mutation resolvers
Look for injection vulnerabilities in GraphQL variables and arguments
Test for batch query attacks and circular reference exploitation

tests.name: Testing REST APIs
tests.description: REST API testing evaluates the security of RESTful API endpoints including authentication mechanisms (API keys, OAuth, JWT), authorization enforcement on resources, input validation, rate limiting, and proper HTTP status code usage. REST APIs are the most common API type in modern applications.
tests.why_it_matters: REST APIs frequently expose sensitive data and business functionality directly to clients. Without the visual interface of a web application, developers may implement weaker security controls, assuming API consumers will behave correctly. Broken Object Level Authorization (BOLA/IDOR) is the number one API vulnerability.
what_to_check: Test authentication mechanisms (API keys, JWT, OAuth tokens) for bypass opportunities
Check for BOLA/IDOR by modifying resource IDs to access other users' data
Test for mass assignment by adding extra fields to POST/PUT requests
Verify rate limiting on sensitive endpoints (login, registration, data export)
Check API versioning and deprecated endpoints for weaker security controls

tests.name: Testing SOAP APIs
tests.description: SOAP API testing evaluates the security of SOAP-based web services including XML parsing vulnerabilities (XXE), WSDL exposure, WS-Security implementation, and injection attacks through SOAP message parameters. SOAP services are common in enterprise and legacy systems.
tests.why_it_matters: SOAP APIs process XML, making them susceptible to XML-specific attacks including XXE, XML injection, and entity expansion denial of service. WSDL files often expose the full API structure, and legacy SOAP services may lack modern security controls like rate limiting and input validation.
what_to_check: Check if WSDL files are publicly accessible and reveal sensitive API structure
Test for XML External Entity (XXE) injection in SOAP request bodies
Verify WS-Security implementation for authentication and message integrity
Test SOAP parameters for SQL injection and command injection
Check for XML entity expansion denial of service (billion laughs attack)

tests.name: Testing WebSocket APIs
tests.description: WebSocket API testing evaluates the security of WebSocket-based APIs used for real-time bidirectional communication. This includes testing the handshake process, message authentication and authorization, input validation on WebSocket frames, and encryption requirements.
tests.why_it_matters: WebSocket APIs maintain persistent connections that bypass many traditional HTTP security controls including WAFs, CSRF tokens, and per-request authentication. The real-time nature of WebSockets means that vulnerabilities can be exploited immediately and continuously, potentially affecting all connected users.
what_to_check: Verify that the WebSocket handshake validates the Origin header
Test if authentication tokens are validated on each WebSocket message, not just the handshake
Check for injection vulnerabilities in WebSocket message content
Verify that sensitive WebSocket APIs use the encrypted wss:// protocol
Test for authorization bypass by subscribing to channels or topics belonging to other users
